title: _gitexec.py and _prunepolicy.py public APIs lack the repo's required header contract and are untyped
evidence: coding-standards.md ('Python (hooks)'): "Document the public API in the file header as `name(args) -> type` lines." Every existing `_*lib.py` follows this (see core/pysrc/_hooklib.py:26-79, a full `# Public API:` block with `name(args) -> type` per function). _gitexec.py's header (lines 1-7) is prose only, with no `Public API:` block, and none of its exported functions (`trusted_git_executable()`, `trusted_python_executable()`, `git_executable()`) carry parameter/return type hints. _prunepolicy.py has a docstring per function but likewise no `Public API:` header block and its exported functions (`plan_prune(entries, policy)`, `protected_ordinal(entries, keep_recent)`, `reduction_metrics(bytes_before, bytes_after)`, etc.) take untyped `entries`/`policy`/`bytes_before` parameters relying on duck typing plus scattered `isinstance`/`type(...) is not` runtime checks instead of declared types.
impact: A caller cannot tell from the header alone what shape `entries`, `policy`, or the environment-derived executable path is expected to be; drift between the two new modules and the documented convention makes them harder to audit for the same invariants (fail-open on malformed input, no raise) that the header format exists to make greppable, and the plugin-side generated copies (plugins/ca-pi/hooks/_gitexec.py etc.) inherit the same gap since they are synced from core/pysrc.
recommendation: Add a `# Public API:` header block to both modules listing each exported function as `name(args) -> type`, matching _hooklib.py's format; consider adding PEP 484 type hints to the exported function signatures (dataclasses already exist in _prunepolicy.py, so hints are a small addition) even though stdlib-only rules don't otherwise require it.
acceptance_criteria: core/pysrc/_gitexec.py and core/pysrc/_prunepolicy.py headers include a `# Public API:` block enumerating every exported name with `(args) -> type`
tools/sync-core.py propagates the updated headers to plugins/ca/hooks, plugins/ca-codex/hooks, plugins/ca-pi/hooks copies
